text: A CCOUNTABILITY U NDER P OLARIZATION * J OSÉ R AMÓN E NRÍQUEZ † H ORACIO L ARREGUY ‡ J OHN M ARSHALL § A LBERTO S IMPSER ¶ January 13, 2025 Preliminary and Incomplete Political polarization can weaken electoral accountability by shaping how citizens process information.
We examine the impact of disseminating incumbent performance information on voting behavior in a polarized setting and assess the mitigating role of a debiasing nudge.

We experimentally evaluate a local CSO’s Facebook ad campaign that delivered COVID-19 case and death statistics to over 2 million unique users across 500 Mexican municipalities ahead of the 2021 elections.
Polling-station-level results reveal that the information alone backfired: it increased (decreased) incumbent support in areas with high (low) COVID-19 impact.
These effects are driven by areas with strong prior incumbent support, prevalent communal values, and higher stress indicators among citizens.
However, a debiasing nudge provided before the information reversed this effect, resulting in voters reward ing (punish ing ) incumbents with low (high) COVID-19 impact.
Our findings underscore how biases in information processing undermine electoral accountability in polarized contexts and demonstrate the potential for nudges to restore it.
Keywords: Social media, motivated reasoning, nudge, debiasing.

There is a large literature on the role of traditional media for accountability. 1 While social media is becoming an increasingly important news source, thus also having an important potential role for accountability, social media has also been associated with a drastic increase in political polarization. 2 Despite a handful of recent studies showing that social media can contribute to political accountability, little is known about whether and how the increasing political polarization associated with social media can undermine its potential contributions to political accountability.

In a political environment characterized by polarization, information disseminated via social media to large shares of the electorate, on the one hand, might continue to be an effective instrument in changing priors and contributing to holding incumbents to account.
On the other hand, increased political sympathies could distort how some citizens process information, undermining accountability.

As developed in detail below, those who like the incumbent, for example, might not update sufficiently from information about incumbent performance that is inconsistent with their prior beliefs, or even update in the opposite direction suggested by the information, thus further reinforcing their prior due to cognitive biases and distrust about the accuracy of the source.
Crucially, this behavior is more likely to be triggered the more polarized the electorate is.

We study accountability in a highly polarized environment—the 2021 local elections in Mexico—by evaluating the effect of randomized exposure to benchmarked information about COVID19 cases and deaths per capita, as well as municipal government policy responses, on voting outcomes.

Especially since President Andrés Manuel López Obrador, commonly known as AMLO, and his MORENA (National Regeneration Movement) party came to power in 2018, Mexico has experienced unprecedented levels of political polarization.

The pandemic also contributed to polarization by giving rise to fractious debates regarding social-distancing interventions, and to stark differences in their subsequent implementation, between incumbent and opposition parties.
We partnered with a local civil society organization, Data Cívica, to evaluate the effect of an information campaign delivered through Facebook video ads.

The ads targeted directly and indi See Ferraz and Finan, Larreguy et al., and Snyder and Strömberg, among others on the importance of local media for political accountability. 2 The rise in political polarization has been attributed to increased exposure to pro-attitudinal news by the social platforms themselves and similar social media connections, greater misinformation consumption, and political tribalism.
2 rectly 9. million Mexican citizens with information about COVID-19 cases and deaths per capita in their municipality.
This information was benchmarked against other municipalities within the same state.
Specifically, the video ads indicated the tercile of the state-specific distribution of COVID-19 cases and deaths per capita to which the municipality belonged.

While voters were, to some degree, aware of policies and COVID-19 case and death rates in their municipalities, it is unlikely they were perfectly informed, especially about how their local area is doing relative to comparable settings, which might be of relevance to judging incumbent performance in a context without parallel.

To investigate the importance of polarization for political accountability, before introducing the information source, a variant of the ad further included a debiasing nudge that encouraged viewers to consider the information without being carried away by their partisan preferences and emotions (anti-polarization treatment).
Other variants of the ad also informed citizens of policies implemented by the municipal government in response to the pandemic.

Consistent with evidence from Mexico prior to the recent increase in political polarization, if citizens engage in Bayesian learning and have no bias in how they perceive the source accuracy, information about incumbent performance should contribute to electoral accountability.

In contrast, if citizens engage in non-Bayesian learning, including confirmation bias or what we denote mild motivated reasoning, or if they engage in Bayesian learning but make inferential mistakes about the accuracy of the source, the contribution of information to electoral accountability should be weaker since citizens do not update as much as Bayesians should because they ignore the information or discount its source.

More concerningly, consistent with some evidence from the literature on misinformation corrections or persuasion through the exposure to counter-attitudinal news, if citizens engage in what we denote intense motivated reasoning, information about incumbent performance might actually backfire.

In the process of engaging in motivated reasoning, citizens might argue against information and the accuracy of its source so strongly that they might update their beliefs away from the direction suggested by the information, reinforcing their prior beliefs.

Alternatively, Bayesian citizens who incorrectly perceive source accuracy due to ideological bias may also update their beliefs in such a way when they believe that the information originating from a source correlates negatively with the true state of the world.
Crucially, the more polarized the electorate becomes, the greater the motive for citizens to engage in such behavior.

While the large-scale field experiment we evaluate was not conceived to disentangle these types of biases, 3 the debiasing nudge was designed to counter a common element of all these biases: the discounting or challenging of a source’s accuracy.

Specifically, before presenting information that pointed to the objectivity and goal of the organization that provided the information, the anti-polarization treatment encouraged viewers to consider the information without getting carried away by their emotions and partisan preferences.

Because the study took place in proximity to a federal election, any information regarding government was in danger of being interpreted as aiming to manipulate the received for partisan ends, rather than as accurate or factual.

Building on an extensive literature on nudges in economics, political science, and psychology, the antipolarization treatment sought to nudge participants to reduce defensive processing or limit their biases against the source accuracy.
These types of nudges have been shown to be effective in economics in the context of education, finance, health, labor, and pro-social behavior, as well as in political science and social psychology.
Notably, Bolsen and Druckman show that warnings to dismiss future politicization can counteract politicization’s effects in the context of science.
Similarly, Groenendyk and Krupnikov show that, if people are primed to be open-minded, they can be motivated to actively process and accept counter-attitudinal information.
Baron further shows that priming open-mindedness affects information processing by shaping which sources to trust.
Also related, Gross argues that emotions can be regulated by shifting individuals’ attention to cognitive processing.

More generally, Eagly and Chaiken find that nudging attention to a descriptive norm influences behavior and Evans et al. show that instructing individuals about specific biases reduces the extent to which they engage in them.
For example, Enke shows that nudging individuals to think about certain information increases the likelihood they take information into account.

To evaluate the effect of Data Cívica’s information campaign, we implemented a two-level block randomization intervention that allows estimating the direct and indirect effects of Facebook ads on voting behavior in 500 municipalities across Mexico.

First, within each block of five similar municipalities, we assigned one municipality to receive an ad with the standard treatment and another to receive the ad with the additional anti-polarization nudge.
The remaining three municipalities were assigned to control and thus received no ads.

Second, we divided each municipality into five similarly-populated, convex, and compact segments comprising adjacent electoral 3 See on how challenging it is to identify motivated reasoning, even in a laboratory setting.

4 precincts. 4 In treated municipalities, one segment was randomly assigned to target all its adult Facebook users with ads that contained no policy information, and another segment was randomly assigned to be targeted with ads that included policy information.
The three remaining control segments within treated municipalities received no Facebook ads, but are considered as indirectly treated segments.

Following a similar experimental design, Enríquez et al. 2024 finds that indirectly treated segments show similar treatment effects due to a high level of social interactions within treated municipalities.

The Facebook ads distributing Data Cívica’s campaign appeared 8. million times to 2. million unique Facebook users. 68% of the registered voters directly targeted through Facebook ads saw the campaign ad on their screens, for an average of 3. times. 30% of the directly targeted registered voters saw at least 15 seconds of the video ads, which is enough to be exposed to campaign content that could affect their electoral behavior.
Engagement with the campaign did not vary by type of treatment, either on average, or for a given level of COVID-19 cases and deaths.

Following our pre-registered analysis, precinct-level electoral returns show contrasting effects of the standard treatment and the treatment with the anti-polarization nudge when conditioning on the content of the information delivered.
Comparing precincts directly targeted with the standard treatment (i.e.
Facebook video ads with information about cases and deaths in treated municipalities) to precincts in control municipalities, we observe a backfiring effect of the campaign.
Unfavorable (favorable) information about relative COVID-19 cases and deaths increased (decreased) incumbent vote share.

In particular, the coefficient of the interaction between the standard treatment indicator and the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths ranges between 2 and 3 percentage points across specifications.
Consistent with Enríquez et al., we observe comparable treatment effects for indirectly treated precincts.
In contrast, consistent with evidence from Mexico prior to the recent increase in political polarization, the anti-polarization treatment reverses the results.
Favorable (unfavorable) information about relative COVID-19 cases and deaths led to an increase (decrease) in incumbent vote share both in directly treated and spillover precincts.
The coefficient of the interaction between the anti-polarization treatment indicator and the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths ranges between 2 and 3 percentage points across specifications.

While we did not have a hypothesis on whether the standard treatment would lead to backfiring or not, these results are in line with our pre-registered differential treatment effects across 4 Convexity and compactness facilitate ads targeting to directly treated segments while minimizing mistargeting of indirectly treated segments.

We observe that treated voters in municipalities in the highest (lowest) tercile of COVID-19 cases and deaths who were assigned the anti-polarization treatment voted 5 percentage points less (more) for the incumbent than when assigned to the standard treatment.

These findings suggest that, on average, voters assigned to the standard treatment exhibit a behavioral bias when processing Data Cívica’s campaign information, while those assigned to the treatment with the debiasing nudge act more like Bayesian learners.
Our design did not intend to distinguish the particular type of biases driving the differential effects between the standard treatment and that with the debiasing nudge.
However, motivated by the backfiring effects of the standard treatment, we conducted additional non-preregistered analyses to better understand the drivers of behavioral biases behind our findings.

While the backfiring effect is consistent with Bayesian citizens who are biased when assessing source accuracy and believe that the information originating from such source is negatively correlated with the true state of the world, 5 we focus on the drivers of motivated reasoning, on which we have additional testable implications.

Consistent with motivated reasoning, we observe a similar pattern of results as in our main results when analyzing the standard treatment effects on the vote share of the incumbent party when focusing on measures of voters’ countering costly behavior toward the treatment information — angry and sad reactions to the Facebook ads delivering the treatment information and turnout — but overall null results when the standard treatment is coupled with the anti-polarization nudge.

Moreover, the results indicate that the backfiring of the treatment information is driven by precincts with higher vote shares for the incumbent party in the previous election, municipalities with higher shares of users with communal moral values, and municipalities where voters were likely subjected to more financial stress and worse mental health.
Our study makes several contributions.
First, we empirically show the importance of accounting for political polarization when studying electoral accountability, which has—thus far— primarily been studied theoretically.

In that sense, the closest paper to ours is Baysan, who studies the effect of a partisan campaign in Turkey, which is a very highly politically polarized context, and finds some evidence of backfiring.
We build on this study by focusing on a non-partisan campaign, which a priori should be less likely to trigger behavioral responses.

Moreover, by randomly adding a debiasing nudge, we can causally identify the effect of polarization on information processing and rule out alternative explanations, such as incumbent responses to the campaign information, likely also varying across the ideological spectrum. 5 Most citizens targeted with the treatment information had no prior knowledge of Data Cívica.
Still, CSOs have been subject to attacks and discrediting by AMLO, which might have led citizens to distrust the accuracy of the information from such types of organizations.

Second, we add more generally to the literature on how social media has contributed to rising levels of polarization (e.g. see Allcott et al., Lelkes et al., Levy, and Manacorda et al., among others).
We provide direct field-experimental evidence on one previously largely unexplored reinforcing mechanism.
In a polarized context, the supply of counterattitudinal information on social media can polarize the electorate further.
This result has important implications for interventions that aim to harness social media to counter political polarization.
Third, we contribute to literature that shows that information provision sometimes backfires.

On the one hand, are among the first documenting that misinformation corrections can backfire, which led to a large literature assessing whether instances of backfiring are an oddity or whether there are clear conditions under which they are likely to occur.
On the other hand, there is also isolated evidence that persuasion through exposure to counter-attitudinal news might backfire.
In turn, we provide evidence that information backfiring can emerge in politically polarized contexts, having important implications for political accountability.
Lastly, we build on work across several disciplines showing the importance of nudges.

In particular, the anti-polarization nudge is founded on previous work in political science and social psychology using laboratory or survey-experimental evidence showing the effectiveness of warnings to dismiss future politicization, nudges to be open-minded, instructing individuals about their biases, and increasing acceptance of counterattitudinal information.

While we are cautious about the external validity of our specific nudge, we contribute to this literature by providing field-experimental evidence of nudges countering behavioral biases in information processing, thus contributing to electoral accountability in elections.
In doing so, we evaluate efforts to counter affective political polarization.
Lastly, we contribute to the recent literature on the role of moral values in politics, and to the literature showing how mental health exacerbates “behavioral biases” that hurt decision making.
The article is structured as follows.
Sections 2 and 3 describe the context and experimental design.
Section 4 then describes the campaign’s reach, before we report our main results in section 5 and explore mechanisms in section 6.
Section 7 concludes.
Andres Manuel López Obrador (AMLO), former governor of Mexico City, won the 2018 Mexican presidential elections by a landslide, with 55% of the vote and a 32 pp winning margin.
More generally, his Movement for National Regeneration (MORENA) party won 69 out 128 Senate seats (54%) and 308 out of 500 seats in the Chamber of Deputies (62%).
However, his route to such a historical victory was rocky.
AMLO lost the 2006 and 2012 presidential elections while running with the left-wing Party of the Democratic Revolution (PRD).
The 2006 loss was by a thin 0. pp winning margin, and AMLO reacted by attacking the former Federal (now National) Electoral Institute and demanding a recount.
To deal with the negative impression that his reaction evoked, in 2012 he took a much more moderate approach to project the image of a more integrating charismatic leader.

In 2014, facing increasing conflict with an important segment of the PRD, AMLO abandoned the PRD and formalized MORENA, which he founded as a social and political movement in 2011, as a political party.
The party debuted in the 2015 elections, becoming the fourth largest political party in the Chamber of Deputies.

Ahead of the 2018 Mexican election elections, despite his political trajectory, AMLO returned to his confronting narrative and positioned himself as the candidate of the people who was running against the mainstream political parties to implement a profound Fourth Transformation. 6 Castro Cornejo shows that AMLO’s polarizing strategy is strongly associated with its electoral support.
Since AMLO’s rise to power in 2018, Mexico has seen unprecedented levels of political polarization ( Moreno, n.d. ).
Figure 1 shows AMLO’s approval and disapproval rates by ideological leaning in 2020, and illustrates how polarized the Mexican electorate has become.
While usually ideologically close (far) individuals tend to (dia)approve the incumbent, the stark difference in approval date between ideologically opposed individuals is the largest in the century.

This political polarization has largely been fueled by often divisive AMLO. His regular attacks, often during his lengthy daily press conferences, on the opposition, civil society organizations, and journalists contribute to political polarization.
The pandemic also added to the already politically polarized context by triggering a debate regarding whether to prioritize the economy or containing COVID-19.
Ultimately, the stark difference in 6 AMLO defined the first three transformations as the Mexican War of Independence (1810–1821), the Reform War (1858–1861) and the Mexican Revolution.

8 views across the ideological aisle was reflected in how contrasting the implementation of social distancing policies across the country was depending on whether local incumbents were aligned or not with the federal government.

text: As a result of a weak response to the COVID-19 epidemic by the national leadership, state and municipal governments had to take matters into their own hands.

We partnered with Data Cívica to evaluate the impact of their Facebook ad campaign on voting behavior in Mexico’s June 6, 2021, municipal elections.

Data Cívica is a non-partisan civil society organization that uses information and communications technology methods to help protect human rights and empower citizens and has significant experience in the development of web-based platforms that provide politically relevant information to individuals.

The campaign provided information about cumulative municipal COVID-19 cases and deaths since the beginning of the pandemic relative to the rest of its state, and in some instances, also local policies implemented by the municipal government in response to the pandemic.
The standard version of the Facebook ad was a 28-second video.
Figure 4 provides an example from the municipality of Atlacomulco, Estado de México.
The first screen of the video seeks to entice viewers by asking whether they know how their municipality responded to the pandemic compared to other municipalities within their state.

The next slide notes that the data to follow partially reflects the decisions of the municipal government and introduces Data Cívica as an institution that believes in data as tools for empowering citizen accountability.

The video then interrogates how the municipalities compare in terms of cases and deaths, before showing relative cumulative municipal data on cases per capita and then deaths per capita up to May 12, 2021, in the final two slides.
These numbers are all taken from the Ministry of Health at the time.
On the left side of each screen, municipalities are shown on a bar reporting their per capita ranking within the states that is accompanied by labels for high, intermediate, and low relative rates.
On the right side, a heat map shows spatially how the municipality compares with others in the state in terms of cases/deaths per capita.
Each representation of the relative case or death rate is designed to communicate municipal COVID-19 performance in a comprehensible manner.

We focus on relative performance metrics within states throughout for two primary reasons: to provide a benchmark that filters out common shocks affecting all municipalities within states, and because the standard public health metric in levels—per 100,000 people—is unlikely to be readily meaningful for the average citizen.
We also tested three variants of the standard ads.

First and foremost, to investigate the importance of the high levels of political polarization in Mexico for political accountability, variants of the ad further included a debiasing nudge message that encouraged viewers to consider the information without getting carried away with their emotions or political preferences.

In Data Cívica we think of data as a tool for political accountability.”; the third panel says “How does the number of COVID-19 cases and deaths in Atlacomulco compare to other municipalities in the State of Mexico?”; the fourth panel shows the total number of COVID-19 cases per capita in the State of Mexico (on the left side we show the ranking of municipalities using a slider divided in terciles: “Municipalities with the largest number of cases”, “Municipalities with an intermediate number of cases”, and “Municipalities with the lowest number of cases”); the fifth panel shows the total number of COVID-19 related deaths per capita in the State of Mexico (on the left side we show the ranking of municipalities using a slider divided in terciles: “Municipalities with the largest number of deaths”, “Municipalities with an intermediate number of deaths”, and “Municipalities with the lowest number of deaths”). 14 additional 2 seconds to the second slide of the ad.
Figure 5 provides an example of this version of the Facebook ad in the municipality of Atlacomulco, Estado de México.
The other variant of the ad also informed citizens of policies implemented by the municipal government in response to the pandemic.
This version of the ad further included a 6-second slide listing the policies implemented by the municipal government since the beginning of the pandemic.
This data, which Data Cívica approved, was compiled by the research team from municipal government websites, municipal government Facebook and Twitter pages, and local media coverage.
Information about the duration of each of the policies was, unfortunately, not generally available, and is therefore not reported.

We enumerate the specific policies, rather than report the total number of policies, because different kinds of policies vary dramatically in terms of their intensity and effectiveness; for example, a shutdown of non-essential businesses is very different from an alcohol sale ban.
Lastly, variants of the ads included both the debiasing nudge and policy information.
Appendix Figures 12 and 13 provide examples of these versions of the Facebook ad in the municipality of Atlacomulco, Estado de México.
Since we cannot detect any differential effect of policy information on voting behavior, we pool the Facebook ads with and without policy information in all analyses.
Later, we discuss why we obtain such a null differential treatment effect.
The data sources for each claim are briefly noted at the bottom of the relevant slides.

Facebook viewers had the opportunity to click on the ad to land on Data Cívica’s municipality-specific webpage that reported the same information, but provided further details about the data, its sources, and the methodology used for data collection, particularly regarding municipal incumbent policies.
Figure 6 shows this information for the municipality of Huitzilac, Estado de México.

To evaluate the electoral impact of Data Cívica’s ad campaign, we implemented a randomized controlled trial across 500 municipalities, including 200 assigned to treatment and 300 to control, randomizing assignment within blocks of 5 municipalities as described further below.

We selected the municipalities holding municipal elections in 2021, which entailed excluding 2 of Mexico’s 32 states, 7 the 2020 census registering a population between 20,000 and 203,000 people, and having 7 These are Durango and Hidalgo.
We also excluded Mexico City because its delegations lack some of the powers of municipal governments.

We also excluded the state of Tlaxcala because early on in the research design process, we could not obtain information on the candidates running for municipal office (the state website was down, and we could not obtain it by phone).
Such information ended up not being central to the research design, but we did not revisit the decision to exclude Tlaxcala.

Although only around a quarter of mayors were seeking re-election, the party-centric nature of Mexican politics still means that voters generally hold parties to account on the basis of a partisan mayor’s performance in office—as prior studies have repeatedly shown.
For this reason, we excluded municipalities governed by independents who were not seeking re-election.

By not defining the sample on the basis of political characteristics, the intervention remains politically impartial in that the sample frame encompasses almost all mid-sized municipalities across the country. 9 8 We also excluded those municipalities for which we lacked information on the number of Facebook users, which likely had few Facebook users. 9 Our initial sample contained 650 muncipalities, including 260 assigned to treatment and 390 to control and comprising 150 blocks.

Treatment assignment follows a partial population design leveraging a two-level randomization structure that enables us to estimate both direct and indirect effects of the Facebook ads on voting behavior.

First, all 500 municipalities were assigned to 100 blocks containing municipalities governed by the same party that were also as similar as possible in terms of 49 covariates, including cumulative COVID-19 cases and deaths, recent COVID-19 case rates, prior electoral outcomes, socioeconomic and demographic characteristics, and state indicators.

Within each block of five municipalities, one was assigned to receive standard ads, one was assigned to receive ads containing the debiasing nudge, and the remaining three were assigned to a control group that would receive no ads.
This block randomization ensures that the Facebook ads are evenly distributed across incumbent parties and increases statistical power.
The second level of randomization occurred within the 200 treated municipalities.
Municipalities were divided into multiples of five segments, which are made up of adjacent electoral precincts.

The segments were created by the research team using the online districting program Auto-Redistrict ( www.autoredistrict.org ) to maximize compactness, convexity, and population equality across segments; minor adjustments were then made to smooth the shape of segments to facilitate ad targeting on Facebook. 10 Using complete randomization, two of the five segments are assigned to receive Facebook ads, with one treated segment being targeted by the ad campaign that does not include policy information and the other treated segment including information about policies.

The three remaining control segments within treated municipalities were assigned to receive no Facebook ads, but are considered indirectly treated because other nearby voters are targeted by the campaign.

We call these “spillover” segments, due to the high potential for social interactions that diffuse information and coordinate voting behaviors and voters attitudes within municipalities—as documented by Enríquez et al. 2024.
Ads were targeted at adult Facebook users in the targeted segments.
Segments were demarcated in Facebook’s geographic targeting maps by a sequence of 1km radius balls that covered all precincts within the segment.
Moreover, we selected the Facebook ad option that allows targeting “People Who Live in This Location,” which Facebook identifies as the place where Facebook users spend most of their time at night.
The Facebook ad treatment entailed around a week of ads targeting treated segments, with the not be sure how far our funding would go.
Funding sufficed to treat 200 municipalities in 100 blocks.

The selection of blocks largely depended on idiosyncratic choices of research assistants and difficulties in the placement of Facebook ads. 10 The exact same process is conducted in control municipalities to ensure that treated and control municipalities do not differ because of treatment assignment.

18 campaign concluding three days before the election. 11 The ad campaign in each segment was funded to reach each Facebook user at least once, based on Facebook’s estimate of the market size for a given segment.

However, given users’ limited control over Facebook’s ad assignment algorithm and the fact that around 80% of Mexicans are on Facebook, some individuals were exposed to the ads multiple times, while others were not exposed at all.
We use two main data sources to evaluate the effects of Data Cívica’s campaign.
First, we use Facebook’s analytics tools to assess the degree of user engagement with the ads on Facebook, in particular the number of unique viewers, reproductions, and reactions to each of the ads.
Although neither user-level nor segment-level exposure data is available, Facebook does provide analytics data for each ad campaign.
We also use information on the number of users that accessed Data Cívica’s municipality-specific webpages with sources of COVID-19 cases and deaths, and municipal incumbent policies.
Second, we collected precinct-level electoral returns from state electoral institutes after the election.
Combined with data from the previous election, this enables us to identify the 2021 vote share for the incumbent party and turnout rate.

Information about the number of COVID-19 cases and deaths experienced across municipalities is from Mexico’s Ministry of Health. 12 We use this data to analyze treatment effects conditioning on the content of the information provided, i.e. the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths.

We use municipalityand precinct-level data from the 2020 Census of Population and Housing from the National Institute of Statistics and Geography (INEGI) and information about incumbent parties from state electoral institutes for our block randomization and as controls. 13 The goal was to both increase the precision of our estimates and to account for the lack of randomness in the content of the information provided, i.e.
COVID-19 cases and deaths.
We use Facebook ad manager to measure the municipal share Facebook users with communal values, which we use to study treatment heterogeneity.
First, we compute the number of municipal 11 The three-day period preceding the election is known as veda electoral in Spanish and political advertising is not permitted.

While the Facebook ads in this study do not constitute electoral propaganda, neither Data Cívica nor the research team wish to risk violating electoral law or appearing to do so. 12 www.gob.mx/salud/documentos/datos-abiertos-bases-historicas-direccion-general-de-epidemiologia 13 www.inegi.org.mx/programas/ccpv/2020 19 Facebook users 18 years old or above we could target with an ad.

Second, we make the same computation but when additionally constrain to the Facebook users that Facebook identifies as having the following interests—due to their behavior on Facebook (e.g. shopping online) and self-reported interests: “community issues,” “religion,” “individual,” “family,” “tradition,” and “patriot.” These terms are those that the Moral Foundations Dictionary ( moralfoundations.org/ ) identifies as related to communal values and admissible for Facebook targeting.
Lastly, we compute the ratio of the latter to the former number.

To measure gender-based violence, which we use to study treatment heterogeneity, we use municipal-level data on the total number of crimes related to domestic violence (femicide, family violence, gender violence in all its forms other than family violence, and other crimes against the family) from April 2020 to June 2021 from the Executive Secretary of the National Public Security System (SNSP). 14 3.6
Our two-level randomization allows us to compare segments across and within municipalities to identify direct and spillover (i.e. indirect) effects of treatment.
Specifically, we use the following OLS specification to estimate average direct and indirect intent to treat effects of Data Cívica’s different Facebook ad campaigns:

The delivery of Data Cívica’s campaign over Facebook was very successful.
The Facebook ads delivering the campaign videos were shown approximately 8. million times to 2. million unique Facebook users, for an average of 3. times per unique user.

Table 1 uses Facebook’s analytics to assess the degree of user engagement with the ads and videos per registered voter, distinguishing by the type of ad (panel A), as well as the content of the ads (heterogeneity by tercile of COVID-19 cases in panel B and deaths in panel C). 15 Outcome means of directly targeted adults at the bottom of Table 1 show indicate that an average of 68% of adults directly targeted through Facebook ads saw the campaign ad on their Facebook “News Feed” (column 1), for an average of 2. times (column 2).
In turn, an average of 64% of directly targeted adults clicked on the videos (column 3), around two times (column 4).
Columns 5-9 provide different metrics of video reproductions.
We highlight that approximately 30% of directly targeted adults saw at least 15 seconds of the campaign, which was sufficient for users to receive some information about incumbent performance.

Such a significant level of engagement with Data Cívica’s campaign is also reflected by the engagement with its municipality-specific webpages providing the same treatment information and reporting additional details about the data and its sources.
Appendix Table 15 indicates that an average of 2% of adults directly targeted through Facebook ads accessed Data Cívica’s webpages (column 1), for an average of 2. times (column 2).
Columns 3-6 provide different metrics of engagement with the webpages.
Returning to the differences in user engagement by treatment and content in Table 1, panel A shows no differences in engagement across treatments.
Moreover, panels B and C show greater engagement with videos showing more municipal COVID-19 cases and deaths relative to other municipalities in the state.
However, such differential effects do not significantly vary across treatments.

As a result, both on average and for a given tercile of the distribution of relative COVID-19 cases and deaths, differences in electoral outcomes across treatments cannot be accounted for by differences in Facebook user engagement with Data Cívica’s campaign.
We similarly see no meaningful differences in engagement with the Data Cívica’s municipality-specific webpages in Appendix Table 15.
If anything, there is greater average engagement with the pages of municipalities with standard treatment than those whose treatment was coupled with a debiasing nudge.

The estimates indicate that changes in effects are linear across terciles, thus justifying our preregistered linear specification, with electoral rewards for high (low) caseloads and deaths being most pronounced for the standard (anti-polarization) treatment.
Importantly, and consistent with our main prediction, the inclusion of the debiasing nudge significantly contributes to enhancing electoral accountability.
At the bottom of each panel in Table 2, we report the difference between the anti-polarization and the standard treatments.

Across all specifications, the difference in the interactions between the treatment indicators and the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths is statistically significant at the 1% level, ranging between 5 and 6 pp across specifications.
This difference is also depicted in the right plots of Figures 7 and 8, but non-parametrically by tercile.

While the estimates of the interaction between treatment indicators and first and second terciles are not always statistically significant, we see that their differences 27 across treatment are at least at the 5% level, when conducting the pre-registered one-sided tests.

If motivated reasoning explains the backfiring, we should then observe that targeted citizens whose partisan views are challenged spend time and cognitive resources to counterargue politically attitudinally incongruent arguments.

We explore this implication by first focusing on angry and sad reactions to the Facebook ads delivering the treatment information, which de León and Trilling show are associated with an outsized prevalence of anger in response to political news in Mexico.

Consistent with motivated reasoning, the results in Table 3 and Figure 9 suggest that such outcomes are increasing with the tercile of COVID-19 cases and deaths in the standard treatment, but not with the anti-polarization treatment. 18 6.2
Second, in Table 4 and Figures 10 and 11, 19 we consider turnout as an outcome, which captures voters’ costly behavior.
In the case of the standard treatment, we observe a similar pattern of results as in our main results when analyzing the effects on the vote share of the incumbent party.

This result, for example, likely captures the response by incumbent party supporters who were not content with the incumbent’s performance, becoming indifferent between candidates, and thus planned on abstaining before the information delivery.
However, when they argued against the treatment information, they regained their relative preference for the incumbent party and decided to turn out to support it.

This contrasts with the lack of differential effect of the standard treatment coupled with a debiasing nudge on turnout across terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths, which is consistent with Bayesian behavior without biases about source accuracy. 18 Since the incidence of angry and sad reactions to the Facebook ads delivering the treatment information is relatively low we consider three outcome for the sake of robustness: the number of reactions (column 1), the number of reactions per 1,000 registered voters (column 2), and the number of reactions per 1,000 targeted voters (column 3).

Figure 9 shows the non-parametric treatment effects by tercile of COVID-19 cases and deaths on the number of reactions. 19 While Figures 10 and 11 report results using precinctpopulation weights, Appendix Figures 20 and 21 report results using as weights the precinct number of registered voters divided by the total registered voters of their segment.
Both approaches yield qualitatively similar results.

To further test the extent to which backfiring reflects politically motivated reasoning, we analyze treatment heterogeneity by the share of votes that the incumbent party received in the previous election.

Among Bayesian citizens without biases about source accuracy, citizens with more favorable prior beliefs should electorally sanction (reward) incumbent parties more (less) likely when a high (low) number of COVID-19 cases and deaths are revealed.

It is likely that there is a mix of citizens engaging in those two types of behavior.
However, on average, there should be more citizens exposed to the standard treatment that engage in motivated reasoning than in Bayesian updating.
As a result, we expect the amplifying effect of prior beliefs to be weaker in the standard treatment relative to the treatment that is also coupled with a debiasing nudge.
In the results in Table 7, we find suggestive evidence consistent with this last hypothesis.
Politically motivated reasoning is not only circumscribed to political beliefs, but should also manifest among those individuals whose moral values are challenged.
In particular, individual behavior is often regulated by communal moral values.

izens vary in how much they emphasize communal moral values, and thus the extent to which they might be affected by the relatively high number of COVID-19 cases and death in their community, especially in the context of a pandemic.
As a result, we expected the backfiring to be driven by municipalities where Facebook ads are expected to reach a higher share of users with communal moral values.
Those citizens have stronger motives to counter information that places their community among the worst performers in terms COVID-19 cases and deaths in their states.

In contrast, we should not expect a differential treatment effect in those municipalities assigned to the anti-polarization treatment, since such values should play no role in the Bayesian updating of citizens with no biases regarding source accuracy.

The results in Table 5 support both of these predictions, although the difference in the interactions between treatment indicators, the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths and the municipal share of Facebook users 34 with communal values are not statistically significantly different from each other.

section_name: Heterogeneous effects by pandemic-related mental health
text: Lastly, a recent literature highlights that stress, and mental health more generally, affect economic well-being by changing decision-making through the exacerbation of ‘behavioral biases”. The pandemic has led to increased gender-based violence resulting from increased financial stress and worse mental health.

section_name: Heterogeneous effects by pandemic-related mental health
text: We, therefore, expected the backfiring effect to be driven by municipalities with higher levels of domestic violence.

section_name: Heterogeneous effects by pandemic-related mental health
text: Indeed, in Table 6, we find that the standard treatment leads to greater backfiring in such municipalities, but no differential effects when the debiasing nudge was also included, although the difference in the interactions between treatment indicators, the terciles of COVID-19 cases and deaths and domestic values is only statistically significantly different from each other when using precinct-level weights.

section_name: Political and local media responses to the Facebook ad campaigns
text: An alternative or complementary class of explanations that could account for the different effects across treatment arms could be a differential response by parties or media outlets when incorporating the treatment information in their campaign messaging or news reports.

section_name: Political and local media responses to the Facebook ad campaigns
text: While we deem it unlikely that this class of mechanism operates independently from how voters process the information unless, of course, journalists process it in a similar way to that of voters, it may amplify the effects of the effects of the campaign and account for spillover effects.

text: Nevertheless, we assess these alternative or complementary mechanisms by examining social politician engagement with and local newspaper reporting of Data Cívica’s campaign and, more generally, on COVID-19.

Our study provides empirical evidence on how political polarization can undermine electoral accountability through biased information processing.
In the highly polarized context of the 2021 local elections in Mexico, we assess the impact of providing voters with benchmarked information on municipal COVID-19 cases and deaths.

Specifically, voters were exposed to a standard information campaign that compared their municipality’s COVID-19 statistics to those of other municipalities within the same state and detailed municipal-level policies to counteract the pandemic.

We found that when this information was delivered without a debiasing nudge, it had a backfiring effect: voters in municipalities with higher COVID-19 cases and deaths increased their support for the incumbent party.
This result suggests that polarization can lead citizens to process unfavorable information in a way that reinforces their pre-existing partisan beliefs.
Our findings also demonstrate that a simple debiasing nudge can counteract these effects.
When the information was accompanied by a message encouraging viewers to consider the data without letting emotions or partisan preferences influence them, the backfiring effect was reversed.
Voters rewarded incumbents in municipalities with lower COVID-19 cases and deaths and punished those with higher rates, aligning electoral outcomes more closely with incumbent performance.
Consistent with motivated reasoning, we observed similar patterns when analyzing additional outcomes.

Specifically, voters exposed to the standard treatment exhibited increased engagement 38 in counter-argumentative behaviors, such as expressing anger and sadness in reactions to the Facebook ads and higher turnout rates, compared to those who received the anti-polarization nudge.

The backfiring effect was more pronounced in precincts with higher incumbent vote shares in the previous election, municipalities with a higher proportion of users with communal moral values, and areas where voters were likely experiencing greater financial stress and poorer mental health.

These heterogeneous effects suggest that motivated reasoning was a key mechanism driving the backfiring, as voters defended their partisan identities and community values in the face of unfavorable information.
These results have significant implications for the role of social media in democratic accountability.

While social media platforms can disseminate performance information widely, their potential to enhance accountability is contingent on the political context and the manner in which information is presented.
In polarized environments, standard information campaigns may not only fail to improve accountability but may exacerbate partisan biases.
Our study underscores the importance of accounting for political polarization when designing interventions aimed at promoting electoral accountability.
Debiasing nudges, even when brief and embedded within information campaigns, can mitigate motivated reasoning and help restore the link between incumbent performance and electoral outcomes.
Future research should explore the generalizability of these findings in other polarized contexts and examine the long-term effects of debiasing interventions on political behavior.

Additionally, it is crucial to investigate other channels through which debiasing nudges operate, including (1) increasing trust in the information source by countering perceptions of partisanship; (2) attenuating emotional responses to information that challenges deeply held beliefs; and (3) reducing cognitive dissonance by helping voters reconcile conflicting information with their existing beliefs.

Distinguishing among these mechanisms would not only enhance our understanding of how polarization affects accountability but inform the development of more effective strategies to promote democratic accountability in the age of social media.
